channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.mmorpg.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.mmorpg.com

item.title: Don't Lose Aggro Review
item.content:encoded:
Making healers look good since 2001

Don’t Lose Aggro is a roguelite dungeon crawler inspired by MMORPG raiding. Developed in Unity, thegame is in Early Access, and is the passion project of 15-year tanking veteran Oren Koren. While thegameplay loop is somewhat unique, Don’t Lose Aggro is still very rough around the edges, but hopefullyKoren can continue and improve on the initial design.

Solo Tanking

The meat of Don’t Lose Aggro consists of escorting an NPC ally through a series of dungeons, wavedefense, or boss rush scenarios. At the moment, there’s only a brief overview of how to play and wherethe upgrade locations are without a story or any real objectives.

Currently, you can choose between a Healer or Ranger, and eventually there will be a summoner class.The Healer will periodically heal the player and has an active ability that does area-of-effect damage.The Ranger is supposed to shoot arrows when you gain aggro and has an active ability that makes themexplode and heal you, but I couldn’t get the Ranger to do much of anything besides teleport around theroom. The player can unlock 3 sets of tanking gear that dictate the default ability and somewhatinfluence the gameplay through passive abilities.

The default set of gear is the Crusader specialization with a whirlwind area attack that takes less damagebased on how many resources you currently have. The next spec is Disc has an active attack that willalso trigger whenever you block an attack and has a reduced cooldown on shield throw. The final is ViceGrip, which doesn’t have an auto attack but instead does increasing passive damage while holdingaggro. I found the third class to be kind of clunky and slower to kill than the other two, but I might justbe missing something.

The gameplay itself feels like a less-refined version of early World of Warcraft. It’s tab-target with anautomatic auto-attack and has a couple of options between the 5 active abilities. Initially, you’ll start offwith a single, damage-focused ability and unlock the rest as you level up during a playthrough.This is something that deviates from the typically tanking experience, as you’d normally enter a dungeonor raid with all your skills unlocked. However, since this is a roguelite, it makes more sense in thiscontext and it doesn’t take long to progress through the early levels and unlock the base version ofthese skills.

The skills include a gap-closer, main damage source, taunt, utility and crowd control.While there is some nuance to the choices, overall player skill seems to make more of a difference thanthe abilities themselves. For example, the Q ability can be a targeted charge or a forward leap, which ishonestly very difficult to aim effectively. There’s also an option between an area or single-target taunt,but the single-target can be spammed more frequently and seems to last longer.

After the first couple of levels, skill choices become options for upgrades, such as providing damagebuffs or blocking/dodging the next series of attacks. Other options seem more impactful, such as makingyour pull ability now taunt everything it hits, which basically makes it an even better taunt.

Rinse and Repeat

Unfortunately, at the moment there isn’t a whole lot of content available in Don’t Lose Aggro. For playercustomization, there are the three tanking classes and two allies as previously mentioned. The actualtanking content includes a single dungeon with two difficulty levels, three levels of wave defense andtwo boss rush stages.

Additionally, as players level up by progressing through dungeon runs or clearing waves/bosses, they’llunlock perks for the skill trees. There are currently 3 skill trees: Offense, Defense, and Support. Theirdescriptions are pretty straight forward. Either do more damage, take less damage, or buff your allyand/or indirectly improve yourself.

I went with the Support tree first, which may have been a mistake since there doesn’t seem to be a wayto reset your skills points, but during my first few runs my ally kept dying. Some of the better perks fromthe Support skill tree include starting off each run with extra levels and being able to take damage foryour ally. However, now that I’ve made it to the end of the more difficult dungeon, I just seem to dieregardless of strategy, so perhaps I should have taken Defense instead.The wave defense mode is what you would expect. Hordes of slow-moving enemies try to attack yourally while you keep them alive. This is the first game mode in the tutorial, but the later modes includemore difficult bosses thrown in. Finally, Boss Rush feels like a reverse wave defense where a raid bossspawns first, followed by some weaker enemies.

So far, I can only comment on the initial boss as I haven’t been able to clear it and don’t know if thereare variations. The biggest problem is that periodically he will teleport you next to your healer and chainyou together to share damage. If you don’t move, one of you will end up dead very quickly, but if you dorun away there’s a good chance some of the smaller enemies will beat up your healer.Although I’m familiar with very similar tactics in MMORPGs, typically there are stronger, high-cooldownor crowd control abilities to use in these situations to keep everyone alive. Therefore, taking on anMMO raid boss with a single NPC and only basic rotation of skills leaves something to be desired.

A Horde of Bugs

While being able to churn out any half-decent game as a solo dev is an impressive feat, it’s difficult tooverlook some of the glaring flaws with Don’t Lose Aggro. If you can name a type of bug, it’s probably inthis game.

In my very first run, I got stuck in a wall that wasn’t a wall. Enemies will sometimes float away and othertimes they’ll explode off screen to return minutes later after you’ve already forgotten about them. Yourally can get stuck in weird positions, although they will usually correct themselves. This one might havebeen user error, but I couldn’t actually get the Ranger to do anything outside of manually casting itsactive ability.

Despite all of that, Don’t Lose Aggro is technically still in Early Access and does deliver a functional gamewith some entertainment value, especially at an incredibly low price point. It does, at least momentarily,scratch that tanking itch and provides a somewhat realistic scenario of what it’s like to tank in MMOraids; it even includes a healer who talks down to you. This is a good opportunity to support a solo devwith their vision and maybe get a few hours of tanking nostalgia, but if you’re looking for a full-fledgedgame it really does need more time in the oven. 

6.0 Okay Early Access Review

Pros

  • Captures nostalgic tanking in bite-sized sessions.
  • The basic tanking mechanics work pretty well.
  • Clear vision from the developer.

Cons

  • Lots of bugs.
  • Lacks content diversity.
  • Some enemies seem overpowered.

Early Access Reivew: This review is based on the Early Access launch of the game, and is not reflective of the game's status when it launches its 1.0 update.

To learn more about our approach to how we review Early Access and why we are applying scores to Early Access releases now, check out this post.

Nick Shively

Nick has been writing about games since 2011 and was formerly the editor-in-chief of another popular MMO website. While no longer involved full-time in the video game industry, he still likes to give the latest RPGs and MMORPGs a spin. When he's not playing video games, Nick likes to spend quality time with his family, battle it out in Warhammer 40k, and play Magic: The Gathering.



https://www.mmorpg.com/reviews/dont-lose-aggro-review-2000137897
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: FFXIV Is Finally Getting A Switch 2 Port In August, With A Free Early Access--Though There Are Some Caveats
item.content:encoded:
The long-teased Switch 2 port of the critically-acclaimed MMORPG Final Fantasy 14 has finally been officially announced for an August release. 

At FFXIV’s ongoing FanFest in Anaheim, California, Square Enix CEO Takashi Kiryu shared the news in-person to conclude a long series of game-related announcements, including some of the basic details about this particular version. 

The subscription situation will be different for this specific port than the others. For all other platforms, one single subscription fee covers all other platforms, so long as each other platform’s version is purchased there (so you’d need to buy, say, Dawntrail twice for PC and Playstation, but only pay one subscription). 

FFXIV’s Switch 2 port will require not only its own purchase, as usual, but also its own separate FFXIV subscription fee. This comes with two slight pieces of good news, though—one being that you won’t need the Switch membership in order to play online (unlike Xbox, which requires the Games Pass). Also, those players who already have an active membership elsewhere, but may be wanting to play on the go, will get a half-off discount on the Switch version subscription. 

There’ll be a free to play “Early Access” soft launch lasting about one month for this version in order to ensure everything’s smooth, followed by the full initial release in August. 

FFXIV Director Naoki Yoshida has been talking about trying to port the Switch for a few months, with a firmer tease around the new year for fans. 



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/ffxiv-is-finally-getting-a-switch-2-port-in-august-with-a-free-early-access-though-there-are-some-caveats-2000137896
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Preview ESO's Night Market Factions, Rewards, and How Your Choice Impacts Your Experience
item.content:encoded:
We know when the Night Market event zone will open. We’ve met the faction leaders. Now, it’s time to learn about the factions those leaders represent (and rewards) in the latest Elder Scrolls Online preview.

When the Night Market opens on Wednesday, April 29th, there will be an introductory quest to get you started within the mysterious event zone. Once you complete that quest, you'll get to meet the three factions and eventually choose which one you want to align yourself with for the duration of the market. Each faction wants something, and all of them want to control wha the Night Market has to offer. When you lock in a choice, you’ll earn Favor for yourself and your chosen faction by completing activities within the zone.

The Glittering Goad is led by Tachien, a Dremora known for her political savvy and it’s not surprising that the faction sees power and wealth as linked. They’re ambitious and business minded, and consider themselves a meritocracy. You’ll want to have a gift for Tachien if you try to join.

Judging by their name, The Ruckus being a faction that prizes fighting prowess and physical strength is no surprise. They’re led by Kharajhal Moon-Eye, a Senche-raht and their ranks include both mortals and Daedra. This faction also offers protection at a price so it seems like they're open to deals and negotiation…but also force.

Finally, the Thousand Eyes is a faction of many of the "small and insignificant” and led by the Daedrat Molto the Whisperer. There are many of them all over, and underestimating them would be a mistake. Especially for their ruthlessness when they have a goal to complete.

When you choose a faction, your choice impacts the order in which you’ll be able to access quests within the zone, but not the content. Favor you earn adds up to rewards for you and a contribution to your faction’s overall total. Over time, you will grow your reputation, increase your standing and get access to the faction vendor, who sells stuff like item sets, Night Market Relic fragments, furnishings, and style pages. 

When the Night Market ends, one faction will win control, so that Favor is also counting for that goal. If your faction wins at the end, then you get bonus rewards like faction monster sets, banner furnishings, and gold.

And check out the full preview to learn some more and weigh your options. 



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/preview-esos-night-market-factions-rewards-and-how-your-choice-impacts-your-experience-2000137894
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
